pins.name: Why They Pass Them By: The Survey Investigation
pins.description: Pin 4 is the investigation arc in which students commit a hypothesis about why big dogs are overlooked, gather evidence through surveys and research, and test common beliefs against facts. The demonstration is a set of survey findings, evidence checks, and conclusions supported by data, and the arc closes Exploration with the Fluency Gate on rate-and-evidence reasoning. The cohort and Guide are the audience, with the surveyed community supplying the evidence. It matters because the profile cards must rest on what the evidence actually shows about big dogs, not on the assumptions the campaign set out to test.
pins.objective: Students will write non-leading survey questions, summarize the responses, and test a common belief about big dogs against the evidence, claiming only what the evidence supports.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student produces survey findings, an evidence check on one common belief about big dogs, and a conclusion that claims only what the data supports.
pins.audience: The Junior cohort and the Guide, with the surveyed community supplying evidence.
pins.pinNote: yearWeek range 6-7 places the heaviest act in the back half of the canonical eight-week window and hosts the end-of-Exploration Fluency Gate before the Act 5 presentation arc.

subUnits.objective: Students write a testable hypothesis about why big dogs are passed over, distinguishing testable reasons from untestable ones.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student writes a testable hypothesis about big-dog adoption and explains why it can be tested with evidence.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 4 survey findings by setting the testable hypothesis the whole investigation answers; the skill that crosses forward is framing a belief about big dogs as something evidence can check.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students turn a brainstormed reason big dogs wait into a testable hypothesis, a familiar multi-step move building on the Act 3 hypothesis work.

sessionTarget.rationale: A modeling session plus a write-your-own production; concept day 16 carries two lesson lines for a single cohort, building on the SEP hypothesis practice from Act 3.

questions.expectedAnswer: Evidence can confirm or disconfirm it

questions.prompt: Name one untestable reason people might give about big dogs.
questions.expectedAnswer: e.g. 'big dogs are scary' without a measure

questions.prompt: What hypothesis did you form in Act 3?

contentInventory.item: Model how to create a testable hypothesis
Distinguish testable from untestable reasons and write your own hypothesis

sessions.title: Testable hypothesis: model the move
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to recognize what turns a belief about big dogs into a testable hypothesis by following a modeled example.
sessions.description: The Guide thinks aloud turning a vague belief about big dogs into a testable hypothesis, then stops before resolving so students see the move, not just the result. The cognitive work is analyzing what makes a hypothesis testable, mapped reasoning toward a standard (Bloom: Understand to Analyze; DOK 2). The artifact is each student's annotated record of the modeled move. It opens the investigation because every survey that follows tests a hypothesis, and it honors the eleven-year-old's readiness to take on a real investigative task once the move is shown.

standardsVerbatim.SEP (Asking Questions): [UNVERIFIED — NGSS practice, not a coded NJSLS standard]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: SEP practice, not a coded standard; first introduced in Act 3 and applied here.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student annotates the modeled example to mark what made the hypothesis testable.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by modeling the hypothesis move; consumed by session 4.A.2, where students write their own.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will accept any opinion as a hypothesis without checking whether evidence could test it.
sessions.artifact: The student annotates the modeled example marking what made the hypothesis testable.

sessions.title: Testable or not: write your own
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to sort brainstormed reasons big dogs wait into testable and untestable, then write their own testable hypothesis.
sessions.description: Students brainstorm reasons people might avoid big dogs, sort them into testable and untestable, and write their own testable hypothesis for the investigation. The cognitive work is applying the testable-hypothesis criterion and producing one, mapped practice (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is each student's written testable hypothesis. It follows the modeling to interleave applied work and sets the question the survey answers, and it honors the Junior appetite to pursue a question of their own.

standardsVerbatim.SEP (Asking Questions): [UNVERIFIED — NGSS practice, not a coded standard]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's hypothesis is testable and names what evidence would confirm or disconfirm it.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by producing the investigation hypothesis; consumed by session 4.B.2, where the survey is designed to test it.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will pick an untestable reason because it feels most true, rather than one they can gather evidence on.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a testable hypothesis about why big dogs are passed over, naming the evidence that would test it.

subUnits.objective: Students learn why surveys are useful, write non-leading survey questions, and plan a respondent sample.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student writes a set of non-leading survey questions and names who should answer and how many.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 4 survey findings by producing the instrument the data comes from; the skill that crosses forward is writing questions that do not lead respondents toward the answer the student expects.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students write non-leading questions and plan a sample, a familiar multi-step design task applied to the big-dog survey.

sessionTarget.rationale: First-teach of research-survey design across three lesson lines (why surveys, unbiased questions, sampling) for a single cohort.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?

questions.expectedAnswer: One that pushes a respondent toward an answer

questions.prompt: Why should you not survey only your friends?

contentInventory.item: Why surveys are useful; qualitative versus quantitative

contentInventory.notes: Must include cost, appearance, energy level per the brief.

contentInventory.item: Sampling: who and how many respondents

sessions.title: Surveys: qualitative and quantitative
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to explain why surveys are useful and tell qualitative from quantitative survey data.
sessions.description: Students learn why a survey is a tool for gathering evidence and how qualitative and quantitative responses each tell a different part of the story. The cognitive work is understanding survey purpose and data types, a heavy first-teach (Bloom: Understand; DOK 2). The artifact is a labeled set of sample responses sorted as qualitative or quantitative. It opens the survey design sub-unit because question writing depends on knowing what kind of data is wanted, and it honors the ten-year-old's strength in classifying.

standardsVerbatim.W.WR.6.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief: conduct short research projects, drawing on the 2023 NJSLS-ELA Writing Research strand; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: W.WR.6.5 strand letters from the brief's migration note; confirm against the NJSLS database.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student correctly sorts sample responses into qualitative and quantitative.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by framing survey data types; consumed by session 4.B.2, the question writing.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will treat all survey answers as numbers, missing the value of qualitative responses.
sessions.artifact: The student labels sample survey responses as qualitative or quantitative.

sessions.title: Survey questions: drop the lean
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to write non-leading survey questions about big-dog adoption that cover cost, appearance, and energy level.
sessions.description: Students write survey questions that test their hypothesis without leading respondents, covering cost, appearance, and energy level as the brief requires. The cognitive work is applying the non-leading criterion to produce real questions, mapped practice (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is each student's draft survey of non-leading questions. It follows the survey-purpose teach to interleave applied design, and it honors the Junior care for fairness, here fairness to the respondent.

standardsVerbatim.W.WR.6.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's survey has at least four non-leading questions covering cost, appearance, and energy level.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by producing the survey instrument; consumed by session 4.B.3 (sampling) and 4.C.1 (data cleaning) once responses arrive.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will write a question that signals the answer they hope for ('Don't you think big dogs are great?').
sessions.artifact: The student drafts a survey of non-leading questions covering cost, appearance, and energy level.

sessions.title: Sampling: who and how many
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to decide who should complete their survey and how many responses make the sample useful.
sessions.description: Students learn how the respondent pool and sample size shape whether survey results mean anything, and plan their own sample. The cognitive work is understanding sampling and applying it to their survey, a heavy concept move (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is each student's sampling plan naming respondents and a target count. It follows the question writing because a good question still misleads with a bad sample, and it honors the eleven-year-old's grasp of fairness in who gets counted.

standardsVerbatim.W.WR.6.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's sampling plan names who should respond and a target number with a reason.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by planning the sample; consumed by session 4.C.1, where the gathered responses are cleaned.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will plan to survey only people they expect to agree with their hypothesis.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a sampling plan naming respondents and a target response count with a reason.

subUnits.objective: Students clean, visualize, chart, and summarize their survey responses, identifying both quantitative patterns and qualitative themes.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student produces at least one chart and one theme summary from their cleaned survey data.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 4 findings and the Fluency Gate by turning raw responses into summarized evidence; the data-summary skill that crosses forward is the chart and theme set the conclusion rests on.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students clean responses and produce a chart and a theme summary, a familiar multi-step data routine applied to their own survey, with the grade-6 data-summary substrate retrieved from Act 1.

sessionTarget.rationale: Concept day 18 carries five lesson lines (cleaning, dot plot, charting, Excel, themes) plus a retrieve Diagnostic on the Act 1 data-summary standard; six sessions for a single cohort.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to summarize data in context?
questions.expectedAnswer: Describe what the numbers mean for the question

questions.prompt: What is a dot plot good for?
What is the difference between a strong and a weak survey response?

contentInventory.notes: Retrieve check before reapplying summary skills.

contentInventory.item: Cleaning data; strong versus weak responses
Visualizing and charting data (dot plot, pie, line, Excel)

sessions.title: Data-summary re-check
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to summarize a small dataset in context, recalling the data-summary skill first taught in Act 1.
sessions.description: Students re-check the Act 1 data-summary skill on a fresh small dataset before reapplying it to survey responses, with a correction loop on what the check reveals. The cognitive work is recalling and running a known summary procedure, plain retrieval with a fix-up (Bloom: Remember to Apply; DOK 1). The artifact is the completed re-check with each student's gaps named. It sits at the front of the data sub-unit because reapplying a rusty skill without a check is difficulty without prior instruction, and it honors the Junior need for a steady footing before new charting work.

standardsVerbatim.6.SP.B.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief: summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: PREREQ_UNCERTAIN: retention of the Act 1 data-summary skill is unknown until this re-check runs; a correction-loop session may be needed if gaps are broad.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student summarizes the re-check dataset in context and the Guide records the cohort gap pattern.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by verifying the data-summary substrate; its output is consumed by sessions 4.C.2 and 4.C.3, the charting work.
sessions.developmentalNote: This is the within-challenge retrieve Diagnostic for 6.SP.B.5, scheduled before the survey-data sessions reapply it.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will report raw numbers without summarizing what they mean for the big-dog question.
sessions.artifact: The student completes a data-summary re-check with their gap pattern named for the correction loop.

sessions.title: Cleaning data: strong versus weak
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to clean survey responses by separating strong, usable responses from weak or off-topic ones.
sessions.description: Students learn to clean their survey data by telling strong, on-topic responses from weak or unusable ones before any analysis. The cognitive work is understanding and applying response-quality criteria, a heavy first-teach (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a cleaned response set with weak responses flagged and a reason. It opens the analysis work because charts built on dirty data mislead, and it honors the Junior precision in sorting good from bad cases.

standardsVerbatim.W.SE.6.6: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief: gather information and assess the credibility of sources, drawing on the 2023 NJSLS-ELA Sources of Evidence strand; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: W.SE.6.6 strand letters from the brief's migration note; confirm against the NJSLS database.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student flags weak responses in a sample set with a reason for each.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by cleaning the data; consumed by sessions 4.C.2 through 4.C.4, the visualization work.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will discard responses that disagree with their hypothesis as 'weak' rather than judging quality fairly.
sessions.artifact: The student produces a cleaned response set with weak responses flagged and justified.

sessions.title: Dot plot: visualize the responses
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to build a dot plot of a quantitative survey result and read what it shows.
sessions.description: Students build a dot plot of one quantitative survey result and describe the distribution it reveals about big-dog attitudes. The cognitive work is applying a data-display procedure and interpreting it, mapped practice (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a completed dot plot with a one-sentence reading. It interleaves applied work after the cleaning teach and feeds the larger charting set, and it honors the Junior satisfaction in a precise, finished visual.

standardsVerbatim.6.SP.B.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's dot plot is accurate and the reading describes the distribution correctly.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by visualizing one result; consumed by session 4.C.3 and the Act 4 evidence in 4.E.2.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will plot values without a consistent scale, distorting the distribution.
sessions.artifact: The student builds a dot plot of a quantitative survey result with a one-sentence reading.

sessions.title: Charting: pie and line views
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to chart a quantitative survey result as a pie or line chart and choose which view fits the data.
sessions.description: Students chart survey results as pie or line charts and justify which view best shows the pattern, reusing the Act 1 graph-reading knowledge to now produce graphs. The cognitive work is applying chart construction and choosing a representation, mapped practice with a judgment (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a chosen chart with a reason for the chart type. It builds the visual evidence base for the conclusion, and it honors the Junior strength in matching a tool to a purpose.

standardsVerbatim.6.SP.B.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student produces an accurate pie or line chart and justifies the chart-type choice.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by charting results; consumed by session 4.C.4 (Excel) and the Act 4 evidence in 4.E.2.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will pick a chart type for looks rather than fit, such as a pie chart for change over time.
sessions.artifact: The student produces a pie or line chart of a survey result with a justified chart-type choice.

sessions.title: Charts in Excel
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to enter their survey data into a spreadsheet and generate a chart from it.
sessions.description: Students enter cleaned survey data into a spreadsheet and generate a chart, learning the tool that makes the analysis repeatable. The cognitive work is applying a spreadsheet charting procedure, mapped practice (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a spreadsheet-generated chart of survey data. It follows the hand-charting so students see the same data through a tool, and it honors the eleven-year-old's appetite for a real adult tool used for a real purpose.

standardsVerbatim.W.SE.6.6: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: UNVERIFIED_CONTENT_CLAIM: this session assumes student access to spreadsheet software (Excel); confirm availability.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student enters data correctly and generates an accurate spreadsheet chart.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by producing a reusable chart; consumed by the Act 4 evidence in 4.E.2 and the Act 6 briefing visuals.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will mis-enter data so the chart is accurate-looking but wrong.
sessions.artifact: The student generates a spreadsheet chart from entered survey data.

sessions.title: Themes: the qualitative patterns
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to identify recurring themes in their qualitative survey responses and summarize them.
sessions.description: Students read their qualitative responses for recurring themes and summarize what people actually said about big dogs in their own words. The cognitive work is finding and summarizing qualitative patterns, mapped analytical practice (Bloom: Analyze; DOK 2-3). The artifact is a theme summary keyed to response quotes. It completes the data sub-unit by pairing the numbers with the words, and it honors the Junior interest in what real people think, captured concretely.

standardsVerbatim.W.SE.6.6: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's theme summary names at least two recurring themes with a supporting quote each.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by summarizing qualitative themes; consumed by session 4.D.2, where the hypothesis is revised against all the evidence.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will pick the one quote that fits their hypothesis rather than the recurring theme.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a theme summary of qualitative responses with supporting quotes.

subUnits.objective: Students test common beliefs about big dogs against the evidence, distinguishing correlation from causation and revising their hypothesis.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student revises their hypothesis against the survey and observation evidence and explains what changed.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 4 evidence check and the handling-cost-versus-bias tension by testing beliefs against data; the reasoning that crosses forward is whether a claimed big-dog drawback is supported or just assumed.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students evaluate whether the evidence supports a common belief about big dogs and revise the hypothesis, a non-routine evidence-evaluation with correlation-causation reasoning.

sessionTarget.rationale: First-teach of informational-text argument evaluation plus a hypothesis-revision production; concept day 19 carries two lesson lines for a single cohort.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between correlation and causation?
questions.expectedAnswer: Things linked versus one causing the other

questions.prompt: What does it mean to test a belief against facts?
Name one common belief about big dogs to test.

contentInventory.item: Revise the hypothesis against observation and survey data

sessions.title: Correlation versus causation
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to tell whether evidence shows that big dogs cause a problem or just that two things appear together.
sessions.description: Students learn the difference between two things being linked and one causing the other, the reasoning that decides whether a big-dog drawback is real or assumed. The cognitive work is analyzing claims for correlation versus causation, strategic reasoning (Bloom: Analyze; DOK 3). The artifact is a set of big-dog claims each labeled as correlation or causation with a reason. It engages the handling-cost-versus-bias tension by forcing students to ask whether a claimed cost is caused by size or just associated with it, and it honors the eleven-year-old's readiness to challenge a too-quick conclusion.

standardsVerbatim.RI.AA.6.7: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief: trace and evaluate the argument and specific claims in a text, the 2023 NJSLS-ELA Analysis of an Argument strand replacing the former RI.6.8; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: RI.AA.6.7 strand letters from the brief's migration note; confirm against the NJSLS database.
UNVERIFIED_CONTENT_CLAIM: specific claims about big dogs (danger, cost, space) are real-world generalizations the brief frames as beliefs to test; treat as hypotheses, not facts.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student labels at least four big-dog claims as correlation or causation with a reason.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 and engages the handling-cost-versus-bias tension; consumed by session 4.D.2, the hypothesis revision.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will treat a correlation (big dogs and a stat) as proof that size causes the outcome.
sessions.artifact: The student labels a set of big-dog claims as correlation or causation with reasons.

sessions.title: Revise the hypothesis against data
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to revise their hypothesis about big dogs against their survey and observation evidence, naming what changed.
sessions.description: Students hold their hypothesis up to the survey themes, charts, and observation logs and revise it to match what the evidence shows. The cognitive work is evaluating evidence and revising a belief, strategic evaluative reasoning (Bloom: Evaluate; DOK 3). The artifact is a revised hypothesis with the evidence that forced each change. It sits here as the turn from gathering to concluding, and it honors the Junior growth in updating a belief when the data demands it.

standardsVerbatim.RI.AA.6.7: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]
standardsVerbatim.6.SP.B.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's revised hypothesis names at least one change and the evidence that forced it.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by revising the hypothesis; consumed by session 4.E.2, the claim-only-what-you-saw evidence.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will keep the original hypothesis and ignore evidence that contradicts it.
sessions.artifact: The student writes a revised hypothesis naming what changed and the evidence that forced it.

subUnits.objective: Students learn the prove-it move, support claims with evidence or falsify them, and produce a conclusion that claims only what the evidence supports, then clear the Fluency Gate.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student produces an evidence-supported conclusion about big dogs that avoids overclaiming and meets the Fluency Gate bar.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit is the Pin 4 deliverable and hosts the Fluency Gate; the prove-it move and the no-overclaiming standard cross directly into the Act 5 profile cards, which must claim only what the dog's evidence supports.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students construct an evidence-grounded conclusion that resists overclaiming and defend it at the Fluency Gate, a non-routine argument the gate certifies to a criterion.

sessionTarget.rationale: Concept days 20-21 carry four lesson lines (good resources, supporting evidence, seminar, support-or-falsify) plus the hosted Fluency Gate; five sessions for a single cohort.

questions.expectedAnswer: Replace a label with specific evidence
Saying more than the evidence supports

contentInventory.item: Identifying good resources and supporting evidence
Seminar: question and defend your claims
Support or falsify claims; claim only what you saw (Act 4 evidence)

sessions.title: Good resources, supporting evidence
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to identify credible resources and pull from them the specific evidence that supports or falsifies a big-dog claim.
sessions.description: Students learn to judge a resource's credibility and pull the specific evidence that bears on a big-dog claim, the input to the prove-it move. The cognitive work is evaluating sources and selecting evidence, a heavy applied move (Bloom: Analyze; DOK 2-3). The artifact is an evidence chart pairing a claim with a credible source and the supporting or falsifying detail. It precedes the seminar so students arrive with evidence to defend, and it honors the eleven-year-old's appetite for real research with responsibility.

standardsVerbatim.W.AW.6.1: [UNVERIFIED — quoted in brief: write arguments to support claims with clear reasons and relevant evidence; not byte-verified against the NJSLS database]

flags: STANDARD_TAG_UNSURE: resource-credibility work may align as well with W.SE.6.6 (sources of evidence) as with the brief's W.AW.6.1 tag for concept day 20; confirm the intended code.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student's evidence chart pairs a claim with a credible source and the specific supporting or falsifying detail.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 by building the evidence base; consumed by sessions 4.E.1 (seminar) and 4.E.2 (the conclusion).
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will accept any web source as credible without checking who made it or why.
sessions.artifact: The student builds an evidence chart pairing a big-dog claim with a credible source and the supporting or falsifying detail.

sessions.title: Seminar: question and defend claims
sessions.tension: When is a difference between big and small dogs a real handling cost, and when is it just bias?

sideA.position: Some big-dog drawbacks are real handling costs that the evidence supports, such as higher food or medication cost.
evidenceSources: survey responses on cost from session 4.C.5
outside research on food and medication cost from session 4.E.0

sideB.position: Most big-dog drawbacks are bias, not real costs, and dissolve when tested against evidence.
evidenceSources: the observation logs showing assumed traits that did not hold from session 3.D.2

sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to defend their big-dog claims with evidence and question peers' claims in a seminar that distinguishes real handling cost from bias.
sessions.description: Students run a seminar in which they defend their evidence-backed claims and press peers to separate a real handling cost from a bias, under the PS1 ground rules. The cognitive work is arguing from evidence and evaluating others' arguments, strategic reasoning in dialogue (Bloom: Evaluate; DOK 3). The artifact is each student's seminar claim with the evidence cited and one revision prompted by a peer. It engages the central handling-cost-versus-bias tension directly and feeds the conclusion, and it honors the eleven-year-old's appetite to argue an allegiance while held to the text and evidence.

standardsVerbatim.W.AW.6.1: [UNVERIFIED — quoted in brief; not byte-verified against the NJSLS database]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student defends one claim with cited evidence and records one revision a peer's question prompted.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 4 and is a primary session of the handling-cost-versus-bias tension; consumed by session 4.E.2, the conclusion.
sessions.developmentalNote: Use the seminar protocol's chairperson and secretary roles to make space for quieter voices.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will defend a claim by repeating it louder rather than by citing the evidence behind it.
sessions.artifact: The student records a seminar claim with cited evidence and one revision prompted by a peer's question.

sessions.title: Claim only what you saw
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to write a conclusion about big dogs that supports each claim with evidence and avoids claiming more than the evidence shows.
sessions.description: Students write the Act 4 conclusion, supporting each claim with specific evidence and cutting any statement that overclaims beyond the data. The cognitive work is constructing a disciplined evidence-grounded argument, strategic reasoning students must structure (Bloom: Evaluate to Create; DOK 3). The artifact is the evidence-supported conclusion that claims only what the evidence shows, the Act 4 evidence. It closes the investigation and feeds the honest profile cards, and it honors the grade-six move from opinion to evidence-bound claim.

standardsVerbatim.RI.AA.6.7: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

sessions.observableCheck: Each student's conclusion supports every claim with evidence and contains no statement that exceeds the data.
sessions.challengeConnection: Is the Pin 4 deliverable; the conclusion is consumed by the Act 5 profile cards and the Act 6 briefing.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will add a strong closing line that overclaims beyond what the evidence supports.
sessions.artifact: The student writes an evidence-supported conclusion about big dogs that claims only what the evidence shows.

sessions.title: Fluency Gate: rate and evidence

durationRecipe.justification: The Fluency Gate runs long because it is the end-of-Exploration criterion check covering the cumulative rate-and-evidence skills (computing and comparing rates, summarizing data, and grounding a claim in evidence), administered as eight short independent tasks so each skill is certified separately to the 85-90% Junior bar.

sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to compute and compare adoption rates, summarize data in context, and ground a claim in evidence at the cohort fluency bar.
sessions.description: Students complete the end-of-Exploration Fluency Gate, demonstrating to a criterion that they can compute and compare rates, summarize data, and ground a claim in evidence on fresh material. The cognitive work is independent application of the cumulative rate-and-evidence skills, certified to a bar (Bloom: Apply to Evaluate; DOK 2-3). The artifact is each student's completed gate at or above the 85-90% Junior bar. It gates the move into the Act 5 presentation arc because the cards require these skills, and it honors the Junior need for a clear, fair standard to clear. (Rendered as a 90-minute session; the Guide may elevate it to a half-day pin event if desired.)

standardsVerbatim.6.RP.A.3: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]
standardsVerbatim.6.SP.B.5: [UNVERIFIED — paraphrase from brief; byte-exact text not retrievable]

flags: BAND_STRETCH: the gate certifies grade-6 rate-and-evidence skills for a 5/6 cohort; the 85-90% Junior bar applies and grade-5 students may need a correction-loop retake.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student meets the 85-90% bar across the rate, data-summary, and evidence-grounding tasks, or is routed to a correction loop.
sessions.challengeConnection: Is the end-of-Exploration gate for Pin 4; clearing it is the prerequisite for the Act 5 card-writing sessions that apply these skills.
sessions.developmentalNote: 85-90% bar for Grades 5/6 per the Forge canon; plan a correction-loop retake for students below bar.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will recall a procedure but fail to ground the claim in the specific data, passing the math and missing the evidence task.
sessions.artifact: The student completes the rate-and-evidence Fluency Gate at or above the 85-90% Junior bar.
